reluctant.definition: Unwilling and hesitant to do something.
reluctant.example: She was reluctant to lend him her car because of his poor driving history.

diligent.definition: Working carefully and with a lot of effort.
diligent.example: He is a diligent student who always completes his homework on time.

benevolent.definition: Kind, helpful and wanting to do good for others.
benevolent.example: The hospital was built with support from a benevolent local citizen.

candid.definition: Honest and direct, even when the truth is uncomfortable.
candid.example: To be candid with you, I do not think this design will appeal to our target audience.

abundant.definition: Existing in large quantities; more than enough.
abundant.example: The forest offers an abundant supply of fresh water and edible plants.

generous.definition: Happy to give more money, help or time than expected.
generous.example: It was very generous of them to donate so much to the charity.

exhausted.definition: Extremely tired, with no energy left.
exhausted.example: After the long hike, everyone was completely exhausted.

convenient.definition: Easy to use or reach; causing no difficulty.
convenient.example: The new supermarket is very convenient because it is close to my house.

curious.definition: Eager to know or learn about something.
curious.example: The curious child kept asking questions about how the machine worked.

fragile.example: Please handle the package carefully; the glass items inside are fragile.

acquire.definition: To get or learn something over time.
acquire.example: Children acquire language skills rapidly during their early years.

advocate.definition: To publicly support an idea, plan or cause.
advocate.example: Medical professionals advocate getting at least seven hours of sleep per night.

empirical.definition: Based on observation or experiment, not only on theory.
empirical.example: The researcher presented empirical evidence to support her hypothesis.

fluctuate.definition: To rise and fall irregularly in amount or level.
fluctuate.example: The stock market tends to fluctuate wildly in response to political news.

scrutinize.definition: To examine something very carefully and closely.
scrutinize.example: Every line of the contract was closely scrutinized by the company's legal team.

hypothesis.definition: An idea that is suggested as a possible explanation and can be tested.
hypothesis.example: The scientists designed an experiment to test their hypothesis.

coherent.example: She gave a coherent explanation of the complex process.

significant.definition: Large or important enough to be noticed.
significant.example: There was a significant improvement in the patient's condition.

capricious.definition: Changing mood or behavior suddenly and unpredictably.
capricious.example: The capricious weather went from sunny to a severe thunderstorm in minutes.

loquacious.example: The loquacious host kept his guests entertained with endless stories.

ephemeral.definition: Lasting for only a very short time.
ephemeral.example: Fame in the internet age is often ephemeral, lasting only a few days.

pragmatic.definition: Dealing with problems in a practical, realistic way.
pragmatic.example: We need to take a pragmatic approach to the budget problem rather than fighting over theories.

cacophony.definition: A loud, unpleasant mixture of sounds.
cacophony.example: A cacophony of car horns and construction noise flooded through the open window.

ubiquitous.definition: Seeming to be everywhere at the same time.
ubiquitous.example: Smartphones have become ubiquitous in modern society.

meticulous.definition: Giving great attention to every small detail.
meticulous.example: The editor was meticulous about checking every detail of the manuscript.

ambiguous.definition: Having more than one possible meaning; unclear.
ambiguous.example: His ambiguous answer left everyone confused about his real intentions.

leverage.definition: To use something you have to gain an advantage.
leverage.example: We should leverage our strong brand reputation to enter the new market.

redundant.definition: No longer needed or useful; extra.
redundant.example: Due to the merger, fifty employees were unfortunately made redundant.

lucrative.definition: Producing a lot of money or profit.
lucrative.example: She left her teaching job to start a highly lucrative consulting firm.

stagnant.example: Wage growth has remained stagnant for the past five years despite inflation.

mitigate.definition: To make something less harmful or serious.
mitigate.example: Good planning can help mitigate the negative effects of the economic downturn.

negotiate.definition: To discuss something to reach an agreement.
negotiate.example: The union will negotiate with management over the new contract terms.

revenue.definition: The money a company or government receives.
revenue.example: The company's revenue grew by fifteen percent last quarter.

outsource.definition: To pay another company to do part of your work.
outsource.example: Many companies outsource their customer support to reduce costs.

happy.definition: Feeling or showing pleasure and joy.
happy.example: The children were very happy at the park.

brave.definition: Showing courage; not afraid of danger.

tiny.example: A tiny kitten was sleeping in the basket.

enormous.definition: Extremely large in size or amount.

noisy.definition: Making a lot of loud sound.

quiet.example: We found a quiet corner in the library.

wealthy.definition: Having a lot of money or possessions.

ancient.definition: Very old; from a long time ago.

modern.definition: Of the present time; new in style.

poor.definition: Having very little money, possessions, or resources.

apparent.example: It is apparent that he is tired.

average.definition: Typical or usual; not better or worse than most.

bright.definition: Emitting or reflecting a lot of light.

confident.example: He is confident in his abilities.

considerable.example: He earned a considerable amount of money.

consistent.definition: Always behaving or happening in the same way.

conventional.definition: Following what is traditional or usual.

creative.definition: Good at producing new and original ideas or things.

criminal.definition: Relating to crime or illegal acts.

cultural.definition: Relating to culture, art and traditions.

dangerous.definition: Able or likely to cause harm.

dependent.definition: Needing someone or something for support.

desperate.definition: Feeling hopeless and willing to try anything.

disabled.definition: Having a condition that limits some activities.

domestic.definition: Relating to home or one's own country.

early.definition: Before the usual or expected time.

efficient.definition: Working well without wasting time or energy.

enthusiastic.example: She is enthusiastic about her new job.

existing.example: We will improve the existing system.

experimental.definition: Based on new ideas that are being tested.

extensive.definition: Covering a large area or amount.

extreme.definition: Very great in degree; far from moderate.

far.example: The station is not far from here.

few.example: Few people came to the meeting.

former.definition: Existing or holding a position in the past.

free.example: Entry to the museum is free.

frequent.example: He makes frequent visits to his family.

general.definition: Relating to the whole, not the details.

genuine.definition: Real and exactly what it appears to be.

global.example: Climate change is a global problem.

grateful.example: I am grateful for your help.

great.example: We had a great time at the party.

heavy.example: This box is too heavy to carry.

high.definition: Reaching far up from the ground.

huge.example: They live in a huge house.

ill.example: She stayed home because she was ill.

impossible.definition: Not able to happen or be done.

impressive.example: The view from the tower is impressive.

independent.example: She is a very independent person.

individual.example: Each individual student gets a book.

interested.definition: Wanting to know more about something.

internal.example: The company held an internal meeting.

international.example: She works for an international company.

involved.example: He is involved in many projects.

keen.example: She is keen to learn English.

known.example: The area is known for its beaches.

left.definition: On the side of the body where the heart is.

likely.example: It is likely to rain tomorrow.

little.example: A little girl was playing in the garden.

local.example: We ate at a local restaurant.

lonely.example: He felt lonely in the big city.

long.definition: Measuring a great distance or time.

quickly.definition: At a fast speed; in a short time.

slowly.definition: At a slow speed; not fast.

carefully.definition: With great attention, avoiding mistakes or damage.

always.definition: At all times; on every occasion.

never.definition: Not at any time; not ever.

often.example: We often visit our grandparents on weekends.

sometimes.definition: On some occasions but not always.
sometimes.example: Sometimes I take the bus instead of walking.

loudly.definition: In a way that makes a lot of noise.
loudly.example: The fans cheered loudly when the team scored.

quietly.example: She quietly closed the door so the baby could sleep.

suddenly.example: Suddenly, the lights went out in the whole house.

journey.definition: The act of travelling from one place to another.
journey.example: The journey to the mountain village took five hours.

knowledge.definition: Information and understanding gained through learning.

courage.definition: The ability to face fear or difficulty bravely.

friendship.example: Their friendship started in primary school.

weather.definition: The condition of the air: rain, sun, wind, etc.

success.definition: Achieving what you wanted or planned.

mistake.example: Everyone makes a mistake from time to time.

opportunity.definition: A chance to do something good or useful.

memory.definition: Something you remember, or the ability to remember.

go.definition: To move or travel to a place. (go ‚Äď went ‚Äď gone)
go.example: We go to the gym twice a week.

eat.definition: To put food in your mouth and swallow it. (eat ‚Äď ate ‚Äď eaten)
eat.example: They usually eat breakfast at seven o'clock.

see.definition: To notice with your eyes. (see ‚Äď saw ‚Äď seen)
see.example: You can see the mountains from this window.

drink.definition: To take liquid into your mouth and swallow it. (drink ‚Äď drank ‚Äď drunk)

write.definition: To form letters or words on paper or a screen. (write ‚Äď wrote ‚Äď written)
write.example: Students write an essay every Friday.

speak.definition: To say words; to talk. (speak ‚Äď spoke ‚Äď spoken)

break.definition: To separate into pieces by force. (break ‚Äď broke ‚Äď broken)
break.example: Be careful not to break the glass vase.

buy.example: We buy fresh bread from the bakery every day.

loose.definition: Not firmly held or tightly in place

lower.definition: Less high in position or status

national.example: Today is a national holiday in our country.

native.definition: Associated with the place of one's birth

naughty.example: The naughty boy drew on the living room wall.

necessary.definition: Needed to achieve a particular result.

nervous.example: She felt nervous before her big exam.

new.example: I want to buy a new car next year.

next.definition: Coming immediately after the present one.

numerous.example: There are numerous reasons to exercise daily.

obvious.definition: Easy to see, recognize, or understand.
obvious.example: The answer to this question is obvious.

occasional.example: He enjoys an occasional walk in the park.

old.definition: Having lived or existed for a long time.
old.example: This old book belongs to my grandfather.

ordinary.definition: Not different or special in any way.

original.definition: Existing from the beginning; not copied.

other.definition: As well as the one already mentioned.

patient.definition: Able to wait without becoming angry.

peaceful.definition: Quiet and free from war or worry.

permanent.definition: Lasting for a long time or forever.

personal.definition: Relating to a particular person's private life.
personal.example: Please do not ask me personal questions.

pleasant.example: We had a pleasant conversation yesterday.

plenty.definition: Available in large or sufficient quantities.
plenty.example: Five dollars is plenty for a simple lunch.

popular.definition: Liked or enjoyed by many people.
popular.example: Football is a very popular sport worldwide.

possible.definition: Able to be done or achieved.

powerful.definition: Having great power, strength, or influence.

primary.example: Our primary goal is to improve customer service.

private.definition: For the use of a single person or group.

proper.example: It is important to use the proper tool.

public.definition: Relating to or available to people in general.
public.example: You can find information in the public library.

pure.definition: Not mixed with any other substance.

fertile.definition: Producing many plants or crops easily.

respective.definition: Belonging or relating separately to each of two or more
respective.example: They both went back to their respective homes after work.

drastic.definition: Likely to have a strong or far-reaching effect

trivial.example: We should not waste time on such a trivial matter.

reliable.definition: Consistently good in quality or performance

unreliable.definition: Not able to be trusted or depended on

subtle.definition: Not loud, bright, noticeable, or obvious.

selective.definition: Intentional in choosing only certain things.
selective.example: The university is highly selective in its admissions.

arrogant.definition: Behaving in an unpleasant, proud way.
arrogant.example: He is so arrogant that he never listens.

decisive.definition: Strongly affecting how a situation will progress.
decisive.example: We need a decisive leader to win this election.

declining.example: The company faces a declining market for its products.

disturbing.definition: Making you feel worried or upset.
disturbing.example: The news report was deeply disturbing.

doubtful.example: It is highly doubtful that they will arrive tonight.

deceptive.definition: Making you believe something that is not true.
deceptive.example: Appearances can often be very deceptive.

predictable.definition: Happening in a way that is expected.
predictable.example: The movie had a very predictable ending.

ingenious.example: She came up with an ingenious solution to the problem.

infinite.definition: Without limits; extremely large or great.
infinite.example: The universe seems to have infinite space.

immense.definition: Extremely large or great, especially in scale or degree.
immense.example: The project required an immense amount of time.

implicit.example: There was an implicit agreement between them.

explicit.definition: Stated clearly and in detail, leaving no room for confusion.
explicit.example: She gave explicit instructions on how to do it.

noticeable.example: There was a noticeable change in his behavior.

protective.definition: Intended to protect someone or something.
protective.example: Parents are naturally protective of their children.

preventive.definition: Designed to keep something undesirable from occurring.
preventive.example: Take preventive measures against the flu.

notable.definition: Worthy of attention or notice; remarkable.
notable.example: He made a notable contribution to science.

outrageous.example: They are charging outrageous prices for tickets.

proven.definition: Shown to be true or honest.
proven.example: This is a proven method for learning languages.

rigid.definition: Unable to bend or be forced out of shape.
rigid.example: The school has a rigid dress code.

regrettable.definition: Making you feel sad or sorry about something.

sustainable.definition: Able to be maintained at a certain rate or level.
sustainable.example: We need to find sustainable energy sources.

deficient.definition: Not having enough of a specified quality or ingredient.
deficient.example: His diet is deficient in Vitamin C.

affluent.definition: Having a great deal of money; wealthy.
affluent.example: They live in an affluent neighborhood.

abrupt.example: The train came to an abrupt stop.

charming.example: They live in a charming little cottage.

reputable.example: Always buy from a reputable dealer.

primitive.definition: Relating to an early stage in historical development.
primitive.example: Early humans used primitive stone tools.

hazardous.definition: Dangerous and likely to cause damage or injury.
hazardous.example: Driving on icy roads can be very hazardous.

indefinite.definition: Not clear, fixed, or defined in period.
indefinite.example: The workers went on an indefinite strike.

polluted.definition: Dirty and contaminated with harmful substances.
polluted.example: They swim in a polluted river near the factory.

desirable.example: Good communication skills are highly desirable for this job.

irreversible.definition: Impossible to change back to a previous state.
irreversible.example: Smoking causes irreversible damage to your lungs.

negligible.definition: So small or unimportant that it can be ignored.
negligible.example: The difference in cost between the two items is negligible.

durable.definition: Remaining in good condition for a long time.
durable.example: These leather boots are very durable and weather-resistant.

preferable.definition: More desirable or suitable than alternatives.
preferable.example: Staying at home is preferable to going out in the rain.

random.definition: Done or chosen without a plan or pattern.
random.example: The computer generates a random number every second.

predominant.definition: Being most common, frequent, or prominent.
predominant.example: Green is the predominant color in this landscape painting.

credible.definition: Able to be believed or trusted.
credible.example: The witness provided a credible account of the accident.

obscure.definition: Not known to many people or difficult to understand.
obscure.example: He made a reference to an obscure book from the 19th century.

inaccessible.definition: Very difficult or impossible to reach.
inaccessible.example: The mountain village is inaccessible during the heavy winter snow.

relaxed.example: She felt completely relaxed after her long vacation.

thorough.definition: Detailed and careful, leaving nothing unfinished.
thorough.example: The police conducted a thorough investigation of the crime scene.

diffident.definition: Shy and lacking confidence in one's own ability.
diffident.example: She was too diffident to express her opinion in public.

disposable.definition: Designed to be thrown away after use.
disposable.example: Many coffee shops use disposable paper cups.

any.definition: One or some of a thing, without specifying which.
any.example: You can choose any book from this shelf.

longer.definition: Measuring more in length or time.
longer.example: We need a longer rope to reach the bottom.

solar.definition: Relating to or determined by the sun.
solar.example: They installed a solar panel on the roof.

written.definition: Expressed in writing rather than speech.
written.example: Please provide a written agreement for this deal.

commonplace.example: Mobile phones have become commonplace in daily life.

increasing.definition: Becoming greater in size, amount, or degree.
increasing.example: There is an increasing demand for organic food.

overwhelming.definition: Very great in amount or strength.
overwhelming.example: The support from the fans was overwhelming.

indispensable.example: A good dictionary is indispensable for language learning.

mutual.definition: Shared by two or more people.
mutual.example: Their relationship is built on mutual respect.

glamorous.definition: Attractive, exciting, and full of glamour.
glamorous.example: She lived a glamorous life in Hollywood.

incalculable.definition: Too great to be calculated or estimated.
incalculable.example: The damage to the ancient monument is incalculable.

indicative.definition: Serving as a sign or indication of something.
indicative.example: High fever is indicative of an infection.

inhospitable.definition: Harsh and difficult to live in.
inhospitable.example: The desert is a very inhospitable place.

dazzling.example: She wore a dazzling diamond necklace to the party.

inquisitive.definition: Curious and eager to learn new things.
inquisitive.example: The child has an inquisitive mind and asks questions.

considerate.definition: Thinking of other people's feelings and needs.
considerate.example: He is always considerate of his neighbor's peace.

compatible.definition: Able to exist or work together without problems.
compatible.example: This software is compatible with your computer system.

conclusive.example: They have no conclusive evidence to support this theory.

diverse.definition: Showing a great deal of variety.
diverse.example: The city has a diverse population from many cultures.

perennial.definition: Lasting for a long time or active constantly.
perennial.example: Water shortage is a perennial problem in this region.

poignant.definition: Evoking a keen sense of sadness or regret.
poignant.example: The movie has a very poignant ending.

slippery.definition: Difficult to hold or stand on because of wetness.
slippery.example: The wet road is very slippery today.

prominent.example: He played a prominent role in the negotiations.

self-assertive.definition: Confident and bold in expressing one's opinions.
self-assertive.example: She is a very self-assertive person at work.

stimulating.definition: Encouraging new ideas, enthusiasm, or interest.
stimulating.example: We had a highly stimulating conversation last night.

preservative.definition: Having the power to keep something safe from decay.
preservative.example: Salt has a preservative effect on food.

quantitative.definition: Relating to measuring by the quantity of something.
quantitative.example: We need a quantitative analysis of the data.

nutritional.definition: Relating to the process of providing or obtaining food.
nutritional.example: This food has high nutritional value.

optional.definition: Left to one's choice; not mandatory.
optional.example: Wearing a tie is optional for this dinner.

transient.definition: Lasting only for a short time.
transient.example: They suffer from a transient memory loss.

uncertain.definition: Not able to be relied on; not known.
uncertain.example: The future of the project is uncertain right now.

unobserved.definition: Not seen or noticed by anyone.
unobserved.example: The thief tried to remain unobserved in the dark.

memorable.example: It was a truly memorable evening for everyone.

faulty.definition: Not working correctly; having a defect.
faulty.example: The accident was caused by a faulty brake.

compulsive.definition: Resulting from or relating to an irresistible urge.
compulsive.example: He is a compulsive liar who cannot stop.

probable.definition: Likely to happen or be true.
probable.example: It is probable that it will rain tomorrow.

selfish.definition: Caring only about oneself and not others.
selfish.example: Do not be so selfish with your toys.

proportional.definition: Corresponding in size or amount to something else.
proportional.example: Salary increases should be proportional to your effort.

greater.definition: Larger in size, amount, or degree.
greater.example: This new plan has a greater chance of success.

older.definition: Having lived or existed for a longer time.
older.example: My older brother lives in London now.

invisible.definition: Impossible to see with the eyes.
invisible.example: The tiny germs are invisible to the naked eye.

irrelevant.definition: Not related to the subject being discussed.
irrelevant.example: That old document is irrelevant to our current discussion.

lunar.example: Scientists studied the lunar surface for many years.

coastal.example: We love to walk along the coastal path in summer.

avoidable.example: Most of these accidents are avoidable if you are careful.

irresponsible.definition: Not thinking about the results of your actions.
irresponsible.example: It is irresponsible to drive without wearing a seatbelt.

insufficient.definition: Not enough in amount or quality.
insufficient.example: There is insufficient evidence to support your theory.

inadequate.definition: Not good enough for a particular purpose.
inadequate.example: The current lighting is inadequate for reading books.

latest.example: Have you heard the latest news about the project?

confined.definition: Kept in a small or limited space.
confined.example: It is hard to work in such a confined space.

continental.example: The region has a dry continental climate.

varied.definition: Having many different types of things.
varied.example: The shop offers a varied selection of fresh vegetables.

allied.definition: Joined or associated by a common cause.
allied.example: They are allied nations that support each other.

retired.definition: Having stopped working because of old age.
retired.example: My retired father spends his days gardening.

unpredictable.definition: Likely to change suddenly and unexpectedly.
unpredictable.example: The local weather is unpredictable during spring.

hostile.definition: Unfriendly and ready to fight or argue.
hostile.example: The soldiers entered a very hostile environment.

exposed.definition: Not covered or protected from harm.
exposed.example: Do not leave the wires exposed to the rain.

subjective.definition: Based on personal opinions rather than facts.
subjective.example: Art appreciation is a highly subjective experience.

unstable.definition: Likely to change, move, or fail.
unstable.example: This old wooden table is very unstable.

collective.definition: Shared by every member of a group.
collective.example: It is our collective duty to protect the environment.

inexpensive.definition: Not costing a lot of money.
inexpensive.example: This restaurant offers delicious and inexpensive meals.

minimal.definition: Of a minimum amount or quantity.
minimal.example: He did the work with minimal effort.

painful.example: His knee injury was very painful.

partial.example: The project was only a partial success.

futile.definition: Useless or producing no useful result.
futile.example: It is futile to argue about this matter.

restricted.definition: Limited in extent, number, or scope.
restricted.example: Access to the server room is restricted.

eager.definition: Strongly wanting to do or have something.
eager.example: The eager students raised their hands.

astonished.example: She was astonished by his sudden decision.

educated.example: He is an educated man with two degrees.

countless.example: I have visited this park countless times.

impractical.definition: Not sensible or realistic in practice.
impractical.example: High heels are highly impractical for hiking.

obliged.definition: Forced to do something by law or duty.
obliged.example: I felt obliged to help my neighbor.

persistent.definition: Continuing firmly in spite of difficulty.
persistent.example: Her persistent efforts finally led to success.

changeable.definition: Likely to change frequently or suddenly.
changeable.example: The weather is very changeable in spring.

defensive.definition: Used or intended to defend or protect.
defensive.example: She adopted a defensive attitude during the meeting.

endemic.definition: Regularly found in a particular place.
endemic.example: This rare flower is endemic to the region.

fluent.definition: Able to speak or write a language easily.
fluent.example: She is fluent in three different languages.

productive.definition: Producing or able to produce large amounts.
productive.example: We had a highly productive meeting today.

qualified.definition: Officially recognized as being trained to do something.
qualified.example: She is a highly qualified teacher with experience.

informative.example: The documentary was very informative and interesting.

tedious.definition: Boring and continuing for a long time.
tedious.example: The work was tedious and took many hours.

extinct.example: The dodo is an extinct bird.

conspicuous.example: He stood out in a conspicuous red coat.

inefficient.definition: Not using time, money, or energy well.
inefficient.example: An inefficient heating system costs a lot of money.

liable.example: You will be liable for any damage caused.

moderate.definition: Neither very good nor very bad; medium.
moderate.example: He has a moderate amount of experience.

relentless.definition: Continuing without stopping or getting weaker.
relentless.example: The pressure to succeed was relentless.

uneasy.example: She felt uneasy about leaving the children alone.

versatile.definition: Able to do many different things.
versatile.example: He is a versatile actor who can play any role.

abusive.definition: Using rude, cruel, or violent language or behavior.
abusive.example: He became abusive when they refused to help him.

relative.definition: True only when compared to something else.
relative.example: The relative merits of the two plans are clear.

intensive.definition: Involving a lot of activity, effort, or detail.
intensive.example: She took an intensive language course last summer.

continual.definition: Happening repeatedly over a period of time.
continual.example: The project was delayed by continual arguments.

deliberate.definition: Done on purpose rather than by accident.
deliberate.example: This was a deliberate attempt to deceive us.

dissatisfied.definition: Not happy or satisfied with something.
dissatisfied.example: If you are dissatisfied with the service, please complain.

expansive.definition: Covering a large area or of a wide range.
expansive.example: We enjoyed the expansive view from the mountain top.

challenging.definition: Difficult in an interesting or enjoyable way.
challenging.example: This is a challenging project for our team.

provisional.definition: Accepted for the present time but possibly changing later.
provisional.example: We made a provisional booking for the hotel.

secular.definition: Not connected with spiritual or religious matters.
secular.example: The country has a strictly secular government.

second.definition: Coming next after the first in order or time.
second.example: This is the second time I have visited Paris.

unsafe.example: The old bridge is unsafe for heavy vehicles.

confidential.example: This letter contains highly confidential information.

consecutive.example: She worked for three consecutive days without a break.

discredited.example: The scientist used a discredited theory to support his argument.

legendary.definition: Very famous and talked about by many people.
legendary.example: He is a legendary figure in the history of music.

forceful.example: She made a forceful argument during the debate.

irregular.definition: Not even or regular in shape or pattern.
irregular.example: The patient had an irregular heartbeat.

reversible.definition: Capable of being reversed or turned back.
reversible.example: This stylish jacket is completely reversible.
